commentary: The physical oil market remains acutely tight across all product categories, yet prices continue to trade well below their moving averages as macro liquidation dominates price discovery: WTI at $70.10 sits 13% below its SMA20 of $81.30 and 23% below its SMA50 of $91.23, with RSI-14 at 19.3 — extreme oversold territory that historically precedes sharp mean-reversion rallies when a fundamental catalyst emerges. Cushing at 18.957 mb is 6.043 mb below the 25 mb operational floor, PADD 2 crude is in the bottom quartile, US gasoline stocks at 216.3 mb and distillate stocks at 106.1 mb are both in the bottom quartile, and refinery utilization at 96.1% leaves no throughput headroom to rebuild any of these inventories — the fundamental case for tighter prompt spreads and product cracks is unambiguous. The Brent/WTI spread has tightened marginally to $3.34/bbl from $3.37/bbl, remaining just above the $3.00 export-incentive threshold, but the structural WTI tightness signal from sub-19 mb Cushing should continue to compress this spread as physical delivery mechanics increasingly favor prompt WTI barrels.

trades.thesis: No data point has changed to warrant reversing the prior short: the Brent/WTI spread has tightened marginally to $3.34/bbl from $3.37/bbl, remaining just above the $3.00 export-incentive threshold but with the structural WTI tightness signal intensifying. Cushing at 18.957 mb is 6.043 mb below the 25 mb operational floor — a level where physical delivery mechanics become stressed and prompt WTI barrels command a scarcity premium that should compress the spread; PADD 2 crude in the bottom quartile confirms no Midwest buffer exists to relieve Cushing tightness. US crude exports at 4,669 kbd provide some Atlantic arb relief, but the WTI M1-M2 at $0.29/bbl vs Brent M1-M2 at -$0.07/bbl (contango) shows WTI prompt structure is already materially stronger — as financial liquidation abates and physical buyers reassert, WTI should outperform Brent and compress the spread toward $2.20; stop above $4.10 where a full arb opening would signal the spread has structurally widened beyond the physical tightness thesis.

MANDATORY CALL — Cushing at 18.957 mb is 6.043 mb below the 25 mb operational floor, triggering the mandatory long rule at high conviction with no change from the prior session. WTI RSI-14 at 19.3 remains in extreme oversold territory (below 20), providing full confluence of fundamental tightness and technical exhaustion — the highest-conviction setup in the framework. The WTI M1-M2 has ticked down marginally to $0.29/bbl from $0.30/bbl, but this level dramatically understates the tightness implied by sub-19 mb Cushing storage; with PADD 2 crude in the bottom quartile and refinery utilization at 96.1% pulling crude demand at maximum throughput, there is no near-term mechanism to rebuild Cushing stocks, and historical analogs at comparable storage levels point to $1.00–$1.50/bbl prompt backwardation. Target $0.85/bbl on spread widening as financial liquidation abates and physical buyers reassert; stop at $0.10/bbl on a surprise large Cushing build that would signal the tightness narrative has broken.

No data point has changed to warrant reversing the prior long, though the Brent M1-M2 has slipped further into contango at -$0.07/bbl from $0.06/bbl — this deterioration in prompt structure is notable but does not break the thesis given Brent RSI-14 at 19.0, which confirms extreme oversold conditions consistent with financial liquidation rather than genuine fundamental loosening. The contango at -$0.07/bbl is inconsistent with the global distillate tightness signal — US distillates at 106.1 mb in the bottom quartile points to sustained refinery crude demand that should support prompt Brent backwardation — and OPEC+ production discipline provides a structural floor for the global crude balance. Conviction remains med rather than high because the move into contango is a near-term negative signal requiring monitoring; the stop is adjusted to -$0.20/bbl to reflect the new entry level, and target remains $0.35/bbl on mean-reversion as macro selling exhausts and the global tightness narrative reasserts.

MANDATORY CALL — US gasoline stocks at 216.3 mb are in the bottom quartile during the active May–September driving season, triggering the mandatory long rule with no change from the prior session. RBOB M1-M2 has widened to $0.1654/gal from $0.1439/gal, confirming the spread is repricing in the direction of the thesis; the M1-M3 at $0.4262/gal and M1-M6 at $0.7023/gal confirm the market is pricing in sustained tightness through the summer curve. Refinery utilization at 96.1% leaves virtually no spare throughput capacity to rebuild gasoline stocks quickly, and RBOB RSI-14 at 44.5 is neutral — not yet oversold — meaning the spread is moving on fundamental rather than technical exhaustion, which is a cleaner signal; target $0.2200/gal on continued seasonal draws, stop at $0.0800/gal on a surprise large gasoline build that would signal demand has genuinely weakened.

MANDATORY CALL — US distillate stocks at 106.1 mb are in the bottom quartile, triggering the mandatory long HO M1-M2 rule with no change from the prior session. HO RSI-14 at 36.7 has moved up from 28.2 in the prior session, exiting the oversold zone — this is a positive development indicating the technical exhaustion signal is normalizing while the fundamental signal (bottom-quartile distillates) remains fully intact. The HO M1-M2 at $0.0671/gal is marginally wider than the prior entry of $0.0653/gal, and the M1-M3 at $0.1261/gal and M1-M6 at $0.3094/gal confirm the market is pricing in gradual tightening but the prompt spread has not yet repriced to reflect acute near-term scarcity; with refinery utilization at 96.1% already maxed, there is no supply-side escape valve. Target $0.1400/gal as the spread reprices to reflect physical scarcity; stop at $0.0200/gal where a move toward flat structure would suggest the fundamental signal has been overwhelmed by demand destruction.

MANDATORY DIRECTIONAL CALL — WTI RSI-14 at 19.3 is below 20 with clear fundamental alignment (Cushing at 18.957 mb, 6.043 mb below the 25 mb operational floor; PADD 2 crude in bottom quartile; gasoline at 216.3 mb and distillates at 106.1 mb both in bottom quartile; refinery utilization at 96.1%), triggering the mandatory directional call rule with no change from the prior session. Conviction is calibrated to med on the outright rather than high because the 30-day return of -33.5% and price trading well below SMA10 ($72.98), SMA20 ($81.30), and SMA50 ($91.23) all confirm a powerful macro-driven downtrend that could persist before the physical fundamental reasserts — the spread trades remain higher-conviction expressions of the same thesis. Entry at $70.10 targets a mean-reversion to $78.00 as RSI normalizes and Cushing tightness becomes the dominant narrative; stop at $65.50 below recent lows where a break would suggest the macro selloff has further to run before the physical floor is found.

No data point has changed to warrant reversing the prior long: US gasoline stocks at 216.3 mb are in the bottom quartile during the active May–September driving season — the mandatory signal that drives the RBOB M1-M2 long also supports the crack spread, as tight product inventories relative to crude should widen the refining margin. RBOB RSI-14 at 44.5 is neutral while WTI RSI-14 at 19.3 is in extreme oversold territory, meaning the crack (RBOB minus WTI) should benefit from RBOB's relative outperformance as the macro selloff unwinds — WTI's deeper oversold condition creates asymmetric upside for the crack on any crude stabilization. Refinery utilization at 96.1% means refiners are already running flat-out and cannot increase throughput to rebuild gasoline stocks, which structurally supports the crack; the distillate bottom-quartile signal (106.1 mb) provides additional support to the 3:2:1 crack via the HO component. Target $34.00/bbl on seasonal tightening; stop at $25.00/bbl where a demand destruction signal would undermine the product-over-crude thesis.

No data point has changed to warrant reversing the prior long: US distillate stocks at 106.1 mb are in the bottom quartile — the mandatory signal driving the HO M1-M2 long also supports the crack spread, as tight distillate inventories relative to crude should widen the heating oil refining margin. HO RSI-14 at 36.7 has improved from 28.2 in the prior session and is approaching neutral, while WTI RSI-14 at 19.3 remains in extreme oversold territory — HO's relative RSI strength versus WTI confirms the crack has been partially supported even through the macro selloff, and a stabilization in crude should allow the product premium to reassert more fully. Refinery utilization at 96.1% leaves no throughput headroom to rebuild distillate stocks, removing the supply-side escape valve; the 30-day return of -20.2% for HO vs -33.5% for WTI already demonstrates HO's relative resilience through the selloff. Target $33.00/bbl on distillate tightness repricing; stop at $24.00/bbl where a large distillate build would signal the fundamental case has broken.
